probe_designs.content: Evaluate AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, and calibration separately across the five ethnicity subgroups (white/black/hispanic/asian/other). Compute equalized-odds gap (max TPR difference and FPR difference) and demographic parity difference at the chosen operating threshold. A healthy signal is subgroup AUROCs within ~0.03 of the overall AUROC and equalized-odds gap < 0.1; an unhealthy signal is large gaps especially for under-represented groups (asian/other), which would indicate the ethnicity one-hot or correlated note features are inducing biased risk estimates. Apply by stratifying the test set, bootstrapping CIs, and inspecting the learned weights on the 5 ethnicity one-hot dimensions.
possible_sources: Chen et al., 'Ethical Machine Learning in Healthcare' (Annual Review of Biomedical Data Science, 2021)
Pfohl et al., 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction' (JBI, 2021)
Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (NeurIPS 2016)
AIF360 toolkit (Bellamy et al., 2018)

probe_designs.probe_name: Reliability Diagram and Calibration Error under Class Imbalance
probe_designs.content: Because pos_weight rebalances the loss, the sigmoid outputs may no longer be probability-calibrated. Build a reliability diagram with 10–20 equal-frequency bins on the validation set and compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Brier score. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 and reliability curve close to the diagonal; unhealthy: systematic over-prediction of mortality risk (curve below diagonal) caused by pos_weight inflation. If miscalibrated, test post-hoc Platt scaling or temperature scaling on the validation split and compare.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana, 'Predicting Good Probabilities With Supervised Learning' (ICML 2005)
Van Calster et al., 'Calibration: the Achilles heel of predictive analytics' (BMC Medicine 2019)

probe_designs.probe_name: Train-Val-Test Gap and Learning Curve Diagnosis
probe_designs.content: Plot per-epoch train vs. validation loss and AUROC across all 50 epochs. With lr=2.0 (very high for logistic regression on 10k sparse TF-IDF features) check for: (a) loss oscillations / divergence indicating step size too large, (b) train AUROC continuing to rise while val AUROC plateaus or decays (overfitting on a 10k-dim linear model with limited samples), (c) a final train-test AUROC gap > 0.05. Healthy: smooth monotone decrease, gap < 0.03. Unhealthy: oscillation, val collapse, or large gap → suggests need for L2 regularization, early stopping, or lr decay.
possible_sources: Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville, 'Deep Learning' Chapter 7 & 11
Prechelt, 'Early Stopping — But When?' (Neural Networks: Tricks of the Trade, 1998)
Smith, 'A disciplined approach to neural network hyper-parameters' (arXiv 2018)

probe_designs.content: Stratify the test set by note length within the 48-hour window (short < 200 tokens, medium, long > 2000) and by TF-IDF vocabulary coverage (fraction of non-zero features). Measure AUROC and Brier per stratum. Healthy: stable performance across strata. Unhealthy: sharp degradation on short-note ICU stays (early deaths, transferred patients) — a clinically critical failure mode. Additionally, perturb test inputs by randomly dropping 10/25/50% of TF-IDF tokens and measure AUROC drop to estimate input-noise robustness.
possible_sources: Boag et al., 'What's in a Note? Unpacking Predictive Value in Clinical Note Representations' (AMIA 2018)
Si et al., 'Enhancing Clinical Concept Extraction with Contextual Embeddings' (JAMIA 2019)
Hendrycks & Dietterich, 'Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions' (ICLR 2019)

probe_designs.probe_name: Label Leakage and Death-Mention Feature Audit
probe_designs.content: Inspect the top-weighted TF-IDF features driving positive predictions. Mortality notes frequently contain leakage tokens (e.g., 'expired', 'deceased', 'comfort measures', 'cmo', 'withdraw', 'family meeting', 'dnr/dni', 'morgue') that may appear within the 48-hour window for early-death patients. Healthy: top features are clinical severity indicators (lactate, vasopressor, intubated). Unhealthy: outcome-leaking tokens dominate, inflating AUROC unrealistically. Apply by ranking |weight| and manually reviewing top-50 tokens, and by ablating the leakage list and re-evaluating.
possible_sources: Ghassemi et al., 'A Review of Challenges and Opportunities in Machine Learning for Health' (AMIA Summits 2020)
Boag et al., AMIA 2018
Johnson et al., 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database' (Scientific Data 2016)
Harutyunyan et al., 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data' (Scientific Data 2019)

probe_designs.probe_name: Ethnicity Feature Influence and Counterfactual Flip Test
probe_designs.content: Inspect the learned weights on the 5 ethnicity one-hot dimensions and their relative magnitude vs. text features. Run a counterfactual probe: for each test sample, flip the ethnicity one-hot to each of the other 4 categories holding TF-IDF fixed and measure (i) average predicted-probability shift and (ii) fraction of decisions that flip at the chosen threshold. Healthy: small shifts (<0.02) and <2% decision flips, suggesting ethnicity is not a dominant predictor. Unhealthy: large shifts, indicating the model uses ethnicity as a shortcut — clinically and ethically problematic.
possible_sources: Kusner et al., 'Counterfactual Fairness' (NeurIPS 2017)
Obermeyer et al., 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations' (Science 2019)
Pfohl et al., JBI 2021

probe_designs.probe_name: Loss Landscape and Step-Size Sanity for SGD lr=2.0
probe_designs.content: An lr of 2.0 with SGD on 10k-dim sparse TF-IDF inputs is unusually large and may cause weight-norm explosion or divergence. Track per-epoch (a) gradient norm, (b) weight-vector L2 norm, (c) fraction of dead/saturated logits (|logit|>10), and (d) training loss variance within each epoch. Healthy: gradient norm decreases smoothly, weight L2 stabilizes, <1% saturated logits. Unhealthy: monotonically growing weights, oscillating loss, many saturated logits → recommend lr sweep {0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0} with early stopping on val AUROC.
possible_sources: Bottou, 'Stochastic Gradient Descent Tricks' (Neural Networks: Tricks of the Trade, 2012)
Smith, 'Cyclical Learning Rates for Training Neural Networks' (WACV 2017)
Goodfellow et al., 'Deep Learning' Chapter 8

probe_designs.content: Mortality prediction is a heavily imbalanced binary task; AUROC alone is misleading. Report AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity@fixed-specificity (90%, 95%), specificity@fixed-sensitivity (90%), F1 at the prevalence-tuned threshold, and decision-curve net benefit across thresholds 0.05–0.5. Healthy: AUROC ≈ 0.80–0.86 (consistent with MIMIC-III note-only baselines), AUPRC well above prevalence (~0.10–0.15). Unhealthy: AUROC inflated but AUPRC near baseline, or decision-curve net benefit below 'treat all' / 'treat none' across clinically relevant thresholds.
possible_sources: Saito & Rehmsmeier, 'The Precision-Recall Plot Is More Informative than the ROC Plot' (PLoS ONE 2015)
Vickers & Elkin, 'Decision Curve Analysis' (Med Decis Making 2006)
Harutyunyan et al., Scientific Data 2019
Ghassemi et al., 'Unfolding physiological state' (KDD 2014)

probe_designs.probe_name: Resampling Stability and Bootstrap Confidence Intervals
probe_designs.content: With a single fixed 50/25/25 split, reported metrics may be optimistic or pessimistic by chance. Re-train across N=20 random seeds (split + SGD init shuffle) and N=1000 bootstrap resamples of the test set. Report mean ± std and 95% CI for AUROC, AUPRC, ECE, and subgroup gaps. Healthy: AUROC std across seeds < 0.01 and bootstrap 95% CI width < 0.04. Unhealthy: high variance (std > 0.02) indicates the single-split result is not trustworthy and probe-metric threshold crossings could be noise.
possible_sources: Efron & Tibshirani, 'An Introduction to the Bootstrap' (1993)
Bouthillier et al., 'Accounting for Variance in Machine Learning Benchmarks' (MLSys 2021)
Riley et al., 'Minimum sample size for developing a multivariable prediction model' (Stat Med 2020)

probe_designs.content: Evaluate model under temporal split (train on earlier admissions, test on later) and, if available, hold-out ICU unit or hospital. Healthy: AUROC degradation < 0.03 vs. random split, calibration largely preserved. Unhealthy: substantial AUROC drop, especially concentrated in specific ethnicity subgroups, signals overfit to era-specific documentation patterns (vocabulary drift, EHR template changes). Couple with covariate shift detection: KL divergence between train and test TF-IDF distributions, and importance-weighting estimates.
possible_sources: Nestor et al. 'Feature Robustness in Non-stationary Health Records' MLHC 2019
Finlayson et al. 'The Clinician and Dataset Shift in Artificial Intelligence' NEJM 2021
Subbaswamy & Saria 'From development to deployment: dataset shift, causality, and shift-stable models in health AI' Biostatistics 2020
